Crafting Your Origin Story
WORKBOOK
This workbook will guide participants in breaking down their lives or business journeys into
manageable, engaging slices and help them develop a collection of origin stories that can be
tailored to any situation.
● Section 1: Understanding Your Loaf of Bread
● Section 2: Strategy 1 – Chronology: Expanding Your Time Horizon
● Section 3: Strategy 2 – Context Switching
● Section 4: Strategy 3 – Interviewing Others
● Section 5: Assembling Your Slices
● Section 6: Putting It All Together
● Crafting Your Ever-Evolving Origin Story
Copyright © 2024 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	Page #1

-- 1 of 7 --

Section 1: Understanding Your Loaf of Bread
An origin story isn’t just one narrative—it’s made up of multiple slices of your journey.
Exercise 1: Identifying Your Whole Loaf
Write down all the significant experiences and events that have shaped who you are today
or the journey of your company. This list will form the 'loaf of bread' of your life or business
story.
Copyright © 2024 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	Page #2

-- 2 of 7 --

Section 2: Strategy 1 – Chronology: Expanding
Your Time Horizon
The goal is to trace back your journey as far as possible, identifying key moments that mark
the beginning of who you are or what your business represents today.
Exercise 2: Building Your Timeline
Step 1: Create a timeline starting from your earliest memories up to the present day. Mark
down pivotal moments that led to where you are now.
Step 2: Pick three defining events from different stages of your life (e.g., childhood,
adolescence, early career). Write a paragraph about each moment explaining how it
influenced you or your business.
Reflection Questions:
1. Which of these moments had the most significant impact on your path?
2. How can you connect these moments to what you do today?
Copyright © 2024 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	Page #3

-- 3 of 7 --

Section 3: Strategy 2 – Context Switching
Consider all the different roles, environments, and contexts you've been in throughout your
life or career. This strategy is about discovering where you’ve used your core skills outside
your primary field.
Exercise 3: Identifying Contexts
List at least five different contexts or roles you've occupied (e.g., student, parent, employee,
volunteer, hobbyist). Next to each, describe how you used skills that are relevant to your
current story or business.
1. …
2. …
3. …
4. …
5. …
Exercise 4: Story Exploration
Choose one context and write a story about a time when you used the same skills you use
today. How does this context reveal a unique aspect of your origin story?
Reflection Questions:
1. What skills did you use in this different context that you also use today?
2. How can you use this story to show your journey in a different light?
Copyright © 2024 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	Page #4

-- 4 of 7 --

Section 4: Strategy 3 – Interviewing Others
Sometimes, others see our stories more clearly than we do. Asking people in your life about
your journey can uncover slices of your origin story you may not have noticed.
Exercise 5: Conducting Interviews
Identify three people who know you well (friends, family, colleagues). Ask them about
moments they believe were crucial to your development or success.
1. …
2. …
3. …
Use the following questions:
● When did you see me first become passionate about what I do?
● What stories do you think best capture my journey or the story of my business?
● What strengths have you seen me develop over time?
Reflection Questions:
1. What did you learn about yourself from these interviews?
2. Which stories shared by others resonate most with your current journey?
Copyright © 2024 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	Page #5

-- 5 of 7 --

Section 5: Assembling Your Slices
Now that you’ve gathered your slices, it’s time to assemble them into cohesive origin stories
that can be adapted to different audiences.
Exercise 6: Crafting Your Stories
Select three slices from different sections of the workbook (chronology, context switching,
and interviews). Combine them to create a short 2-3 minute version of your origin story.
***Write this story down and practice telling it out loud.
Exercise 7: Tailoring for Your Audience
Consider three different audiences you might share your origin story with (e.g., potential
investors, clients, podcast listeners). Write down how you might adjust your story for each
audience, using different slices as needed.
1. …
2. …
3. …
Reflection Questions:
1. Which slices feel the most authentic or powerful?
2. How did you adapt your story to fit different audiences?
Copyright © 2024 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	Page #6

-- 6 of 7 --

Section 6: Putting It All Together
Exercise 8: Your Origin Story Arsenal
Using all the slices you've collected, create a master list of origin stories that you can pull
from when needed. Identify which stories are best suited for different situations (e.g.,
networking events, interviews, marketing materials).
Bonus Exercise: Storytelling Practice
Choose one story from your list and share it with a friend or colleague. Ask for feedback on
what resonated and how you might refine it further.
Crafting Your Ever-Evolving Origin Story
Remember, your origin story isn’t static—it’s a living narrative that evolves as you do.
Continue to gather new slices, refine your stories, and adapt them to different audiences.
